next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

Mira was copying a rib scar into the registry when the front dryers lost their steady coin-fed thump.

The ledger lay open on a flour-sack towel between her and Lin, thick now with handwriting from five different pens, its spine already giving up under the damp. Lin had organized the latest pages by the things digital systems liked to erase first: names no contract had bothered to keep, repeated fractures, serial-port marks, the little bodily facts that made a person harder to pulp into a defect code. Across from Mira, Lin held the book flat with the heel of one hand, their lighter iris flashing pale whenever they looked up into the fluorescent buzz. They had started licking the pencil tip like Jo when the page dragged. Every time they did it they made the same offended face, because graphite tasted like battery dust.

"Read that line again," Mira said.

Lin bent over the previous page. "Left femur spiral set, old, healed wrong. Smelled bleach whenever the memory came up. Said the donor liked sugared fennel after lunch."

"Not the smell. The address."

"Unit eighteen, Marrow Street, over a bakery that ran hot at four in the morning."

Mira wrote it down. The pencil lead snapped. She swore softly and dug the broken point out of the paper with her nail.

The entry belonged to a woman who had spent forty dollars she did not have to find out why her rented hostess body kept waking up with somebody else's limp. Three pages earlier the same crooked femur had turned up in a bargain courier frame. Two pages before that the bakery smell had come through a sex-work leas...
open_hooks_to_carry: Show the desk operating as work rather than montage.
Make handwritten documentation a meaningful tactical choice.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e her work. Overhead, the red numbers rolled to the next case.

At the security desk a woman with chipped bronze nail polish returned Mira's pocketknife after logging it out of the morning tray, then hesitated and kept the badge on her side of the glass. "Company property," she said, apologetic because apology was free.

Outside, the civic-core steps shone with old rain. The afternoon had turned bright in that mean autumn way that made every puddle look metallic. Ferry horns moved somewhere beyond the buildings. A tram bell clanged on the avenue. The heat of the hall left her all at once, replaced by wind off the water pushing through her blouse where the badge clip had been.

The forms in her hand were already softening at the corners from sweat. Suspension. Fine. Termination pending final processing. Each word did its own small separate harm. She could picture the payroll deposit that would not land on Friday. Could picture Gabe saying he was fine too quickly. Could picture Brine Court Housing's subject line in her inbox and the way the auto-generated courtesy always sounded most insulting right before eviction.

Behind the glass doors, Yaret was already turning back toward the interior as if she had never required more than a signature. Celia was nowhere visible. Good. Mira did not want to see whether she had stayed to work or to watch.

She folded the forms once and put them in her bag with the lunch and the wafer and the painkillers, all of it the same weight now. Then she went down the steps before the guard inside decided that even standing there counted as trespass.
state_deltas_required: Without salary or legal cover, the Blue Hour crew must keep people repaired while turning scattered memory fragments into something durable enough to survive seizure and disbelief.
The mission shifts from exposing corporate wrongdoing to building a registry of stolen selves and nonconsenting donors.
